title: Water conservation through plumbing and nudging
data_sources: PUB Singapore monthly water billing records
Administrative population records (2.8 M adult Singaporeans)

data_scope.spatial_coverage: Singapore (single city) — all public HDB housing, 10,188 blocks
data_scope.treatment_units: 2,262 HDB blocks / ~360,000 flats completing HIP by December 2019

core_conclusion: A nationwide plumbing upgrade programme (HIP) in Singapore causally reduced residential water consumption by 3.5% — an effect that persists over a decade and is robust across demographic subgroups. A nationwide peer-comparison nudging programme, however, produced no net reduction: boomerang increases among low-usage households cancelled savings from high-usage households. Efficiency improvements also amplified other conservation policies and buffered demand against extreme heat and air pollution.
why_it_matters: Largest causal evaluation of residential plumbing efficiency on record (98 M obs.); quantifies real-world behavioural rebound that reduces engineering estimates; exposes structural failure of national-scale nudging from boomerang effects; demonstrates that infrastructure efficiency multiplies other policy impacts; reframes cost-benefit analysis by including housing-value appreciation.
why_nature: Behavioural science at national scale matches Nature Human Behaviour's core mandate; high causal rigour (staggered DiD, RD, Bayes factors) at exceptional statistical power; interdisciplinary span across environmental economics, behavioural economics and urban policy; counterintuitive null nudging result provides high-value corrective evidence.
added_value_to_my_research: Staggered DiD causal template transferable to greenspace/heat interventions with spatially heterogeneous effects — extends my MGWR/GWR spatial heterogeneity toolkit
Boomerang-effect characterisation motivates explainable-ML precision targeting: SHAP values predict which households will respond adversely before campaign deployment
Water-equity distributional analysis parallels 2SFCA equity audits — block-level water-cost burden maps analogous to healthcare accessibility scores
Multi-utility billing fusion (water + electricity) creates a clean anonymized test-bed for physics-informed graph learning coupling household water and energy consumption
Cross-city replication framework: the paper's regression specifications serve as a structural prior in a hierarchical Bayesian pooling model for multi-city governance comparisons

related.title: Water conservation benefits of urban heat mitigation
related.relation: water-heat interaction; extends the paper's heat-buffering finding to urban cooling strategies

related.title: Large and persistent effects of green energy defaults in the household and business sectors
related.relation: parallel behavioural intervention (defaults vs. nudges) at large scale; contrast with null nudging result

related.title: Electrical appliances moderate households' water demand response to heat
related.relation: energy-water nexus in Singapore residential context; directly extends the paper's heat-water interaction

related.title: Urban inequality, the housing crisis and deteriorating water access in US cities
related.relation: equity dimension of urban water access; motivates distributional welfare extension of the paper

related.title: Intercity personnel exchange is more effective than policy transplantation at reducing water pollution
related.relation: cross-city water governance comparison; motivates external-validity replication of Singapore findings

figures.caption: Trend of monthly water consumption for non-HIP, pre-HIP and post-HIP flats (98.3 M obs.) — validates parallel pre-trends assumption
Heterogeneous HIP effects across flat type, age, ownership, water demand quartile and block-level demographics (11 panels)
Event study — annual HIP effect over ±10 years showing immediate and persistent consumption reduction
Differential policy interaction: post-HIP flats respond more strongly to peer comparison and price-increase policies
HIP moderates water demand spikes from high temperature, excessive rainfall and unhealthy PSI (3 panels)
Nudging effects by baseline consumption decile and norm-message type — boomerang effects cancel net conservation
Heterogeneous nudging effects by message received, consumption decile, flat type and HIP status (12 sub-panels)
